elements.sent-2411-002: We present the in-lab and on-sky performance for the upgraded 90~GHz focal plane of the Cosmology Large Angular Scale Surveyor (CLASS), which had four of its seven detector wafers updated during the austral winter of 2022.
elements.sent-2411-003: The update aimed to improve the transition-edge-sensor (TES) stability and bias range and to realize the high optical efficiency of the sensor design.
elements.sent-2411-004: Modifications included revised circuit terminations, electrical contact between the TES superconductor and the normal metal providing the bulk of the bolometer’s heat capacity, and additional filtering on the TES bias lines.
elements.sent-2411-005: The upgrade was successful: 94\% of detectors are stable down to 15\% of the normal resistance, providing a wide overlapping range of bias voltages for all TESs on a wafer.
elements.sent-2411-006: The median telescope efficiency improved from $0.42^{+0.15 _{-0.22}$ to $0.60^{+0.10 _{-0.32}$ (68\% quantiles).}}
elements.sent-2411-007: For the four upgraded wafers alone, median telescope efficiency increased to $0.65^{+0.06 _{-0.06}$.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Given our efficiency estimate for the receiver optics, this telescope efficiency implies a detector efficiency exceeding $0.90$.
elements.sent-2411-009: The overall noise-equivalent temperature of the \unit{90 {GHz focal plane improved from \unit{19 {\mu K\sqrt{s to \unit{11.3 {\mu K\sqrt{s .}}}}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \subfloat[]{\includegraphics[width=2.8in]{figures/W2_in_field_labeled_small.pdf}\label{subfig:W1_in_field}} \hfil \subfloat[]{ \includegraphics[width=2.8in]{figures/w-band-detector-wafer_white.pdf}\label{subfig:w-band-detector-wafer_white} } \subfloat[]{\includegraphics[width=2.8in]{figures/revised_pixel_v2.pdf} \label{subfig:revised_pixel}}\hfil \subfloat[]{\includegraphics[width=2.8in]{figures/TES_architecture_V2.pdf} \label{subfig:TES_architecture}} \caption{(a) Photograph of the upgraded \unit{90}{GHz} focal plane, taken at the CLASS site. The four new modules are outlined in blue. Thirty-seven copper feedhorns per module couple detectors to the incoming radiation. (b) Within each module is an indium-bonded stack of silicon wafers into which is built an integrated array of thirty-seven dual-polarization detector pixels. (c) A single pixel shows updated components: the terminated vialess crossover, the magic tee, and the TES. (d) Upgraded CLASS TES showing three primary design changes described in Section~\ref{sec:design}: 1) a revised termination with a resistive PdAu meander that is compact and robust against dimensional and alignment tolerances encountered during fabrication; 2) a direct normal-metal contact between the MoAu TES and the Pd heat capacity element; 3) the revised choke filter circuit implementation extending onto the membrane's diffusive bias-lead legs. Images are reproduced from \citetalias{nunez22spie,nunez23asc}. }
elements.sent-2411-010: Measurements of the cosmic microwave background (CMB) have provided the tightest constraints to date on the parameters of the $\Lambda$CDM cosmological model and its extensions \citep[e.g.,][]{bennett13,hinshaw13,planck18VI,BK21 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: For the past twenty years, advances in the sensitivity of CMB measurements have been enabled in a large part by advances in cryogenic detector technology, with transition-edge-sensor (TES) bolometers playing a central role \cite[e.g.,][]{niemack08,kuo08,shirokoff10,arnold12,suzuki12,rostem12spie,appel14spie,posada16,hui16 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Along these lines, this paper concerns improvements to cryogenic detector performance for the Cosmology Large Angular Scale Survey (CLASS) project.
elements.sent-2411-013: CLASS aims to measure CMB polarization on the largest angular scales ($\ell<30$) to constrain the optical depth of reionization and primordial gravitational radiation \citep{essinger-hileman14spie,watts15,harrington16spie,watts18 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: This is one of the frontiers of the CMB field, with associated projects pursuing complementary strategies \citep{litebird22,piper14,lspe20,groundbird20,lspe20, quijote23, may2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Major upcoming international-scale ground-based surveys will constrain primordial gravitational waves and measure the growth of cosmic structure on smaller angular scales \citep{simons19whitepaper,stagefour22bmodeforecast .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Located on Cerro Toco at \unit{5200 {m in the Chilean Atacama Desert, the CLASS polarization-sensitive array of microwave telescopes operates in frequency bands centered approximately on 40, 90, 150, and \unit{220 {GHz \citep{eimer12spie,appel14spie,harrington18spie,dahal18spie .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Among these, the \unit{90 {GHz band is closest to the minimum in polarized Galactic emission \citep[][Figure 51]{planck15V , and the nominal CLASS design has two dedicated \unit{90 {GHz telescopes.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, the initial run of \unit{90 {GHz detectors, which concluded in 2018, yielded only one telescope's detectors with problematic variation in stable bias range and optical efficiency \citep[][hereafter \citetalias{dahal22 ]{dahal22 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: To improve detector performance we updated the detector design and replaced part of the first 90 GHz telescope’s detectors with the upgraded version in August 2022.
elements.sent-2411-020: Initial characterizations of the new detectors have been reported in \citet{nunez22spie,nunez23asc , hereafter \citetalias{nunez22spie,nunez23asc .}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \subfloat[]{\includegraphics[height=2.5in]{figures/labeled_magictee_v3.pdf}} \label{subfig:magic_tee} \hfil \subfloat[]{\includegraphics[height=2.5in]{figures/labeled_crossover_v3.pdf}} \label{subfig:crossover} \caption{The magic tee (a) and terminated vialess crossover (b) with the revised PdAu circuit termination. The new meandered PdAu termination was realized as a lossy stepped impedance transition between Nb to PdAu. This design is robust against fabrication variability and leads to a reduction of the total meander length and device footprint. Images are reproduced from \citetalias{nunez23asc}.} \label{fig:redesign}
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper, we present the full in-lab and on-sky characterization of the upgraded \unit{90 {GHz focal plane.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: We discuss the design of the upgraded detector wafers in Section~\ref{sec:design , emphasizing updates.}
elements.sent-2411-023: The types of data for detector characterization are described in Section~\ref{sec:data .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Sections~\ref{sec:electrothermal --\ref{sec:stability cover the electrothermal TES parameters, bandpass, optical efficiency, noise performance, and stability.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Finally, in Section~\ref{sec:conclusion we summarize the results, while spatial variations in electrothermal properties and optical efficiency are covered in Appendix~\ref{app:Detector Property Spatial Variation .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Figure \ref{subfig:W1_in_field shows a photograph of the four upgraded detector modules installed in the 90~GHz focal plane in July 2022.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In total, the detector array holds seven modules, and those replaced were the least-well-performing modules in the first 90~GHz detector array (W1).
elements.sent-2411-028: The most apparent feature of the modules in the photo is the thirty-seven copper feedhorns that couple the incoming radiation to the detector pixels inside the module \citep{zeng10spie,dahal18spie .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Visible behind the feedhorns is the gold-plated aluminum-silicon alloy interface plate \citep{Ali-CE7 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Mounted to the back of this plate is an indium-bonded and gold-plated stack of silicon wafers that provides broadband optical coupling and out-of-band light rejection for the detector wafer shown in Figure 1b.
elements.sent-2411-031: Orthogonal polarized signals received via the feedhorn are diplexed by a symmetric waveguide orthomode transducer (OMT) implemented in planar circuitry and then subsequently filtered and detected on-chip.
elements.sent-2411-032: The detector wafer integrates thirty-seven dual-polarization detector pixels.
elements.sent-2411-033: The multi-layer detector stack is fastened and aligned with the interface and feedhorns through micromachined holes.
elements.sent-2411-034: In addition to the seventy-four TESs associated with detector pixels (one per polarization per pixel), several optically isolated ``dark TES'' bolometers are used to assess parasitic power loading.
elements.sent-2411-035: A TES is voltage-biased and read out through a pair of leads that connect to wirebond pads on one side of the wafer.
elements.sent-2411-036: Additional cryogenic module circuitry comprises shunt-resistor chips to provide voltage biases and time-division multiplexing chips to read the detector current \citep{reintsema03,nist_tdm_mux13b .}
elements.sent-2411-037: The detector modules are controlled and read out with warm Multi-Channel Electronics (MCE) \citep{Battistelli08 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Figure~\ref{subfig:revised_pixel shows the upgraded detector pixel, which inherits most of its design from the original W1 detectors.}
elements.sent-2411-039: For a full description of the original CLASS 90~GHz module and detector design, see~\cite{Denis-fabrication, Chuss-development, rostem16spie, dahal18spie .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Here we provide an overview and highlight design elements that changed in the upgraded detectors.
elements.sent-2411-041: Aside from terminations, all microwave circuitry on the detector wafer is made from superconducting niobium with a dielectric of high-purity single-crystal silicon derived from the \unit{5 {\mu m -thick device layer of a silicon-on-insulator (SOI) wafer.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: A pair of symmetric antenna probes couple orthogonal polarization states of incoming light arriving through a circular waveguide that runs from the base of the feedhorn through the interface plate and the initial layers of the detector wafer stack.
elements.sent-2411-043: A waveguide quarter-wave backshort behind the detector wafer enhances the coupling \citep{Crowe-choke .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Radiation picked up by opposing sides of an antenna-probe pair transitions from co-planar waveguide to microstrip transmission line and, depending on the side, either passes the orthogonal probe's line by a vialess crossover~\citep{Uyen2009 or passes an impedance-matched ``dummy'' terminated vialess crossover, which symmetrizes the circuit response between both signal paths.}
elements.sent-2411-045: The radiation from opposing probe antennas is then coherently combined at the magic tee, which transmits the difference and terminates the sum signal \citep{U-Yen-magicT .}
elements.sent-2411-046: The signal transmitted by the magic tee propagates through microstrip low-pass and band-defining filters before terminating at the corresponding TES bolometer.
elements.sent-2411-047: Terminations for the via-less crossovers and the magic-tees on the original 90 GHz detector arrays were realized in a microstrip configuration by over laying a superconducting Nb line with a wider resistive Au layer shaped to provide adiabatic absorption along the structure and impedance matching.
elements.sent-2411-048: Due to concerns that termination performance was degraded by proximitization of the Au \citep{proximity2, proximity1 , the terminations for the upgraded pixel were redesigned.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The new terminations are shown in Figure~\ref{fig:redesign .}
elements.sent-2411-050: They feature a stepped-impedance transformer from superconducting Nb to normal PdAu.
elements.sent-2411-051: The larger density of states, reduced diffusivity, and strong Pauli paramagnetism of Pd combine to limit proximitization of the PdAu compared with Au.
elements.sent-2411-052: The PdAu is a disordered alloy (RRR=1.1) with a relatively high electrical bulk resistivity, which enables a reduction in the termination footprint.
elements.sent-2411-053: Finally, the stepped-impedance design is more robust against dimensional variations and misalignment in fabrication as compared with the original tapered design.
elements.sent-2411-054: Figure \ref{subfig:TES_architecture shows the TES bolometer in detail.}
elements.sent-2411-055: The signal enters by the tapered Nb microstrip.
elements.sent-2411-056: This transmission line crosses a short Si beam, which dominates the thermal conductance between the bolometer and the rest of the detector wafer \citep{rostem-ballistic .}
elements.sent-2411-057: The width of the beam was targeted (measured) to be \unit{10 {\mu m (9.5--\unit{9.7 {\mu m ), which was chosen to provide \unit{13.2 {pW of cooling given the target TES critical temperature and wafer bath temperature.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Additional details of the thermal conductance device parametrization are discussed in Section~\ref{sec:electrothermal .}
elements.sent-2411-059: The short Si beam and bias-lead legs suspend the TES island, which is also \unit{5 {\mu m -thick being built from the device layer.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: On this island live three components that were part of the detector upgrade: (1) the signal termination, (2) the Pd thermal mass, and (3) the MoAu superconducting bilayer.
elements.sent-2411-061: The island temperature is maintained at the critical temperature $\Tc$ of the MoAu bilayer by power from the signal termination and the voltage bias maintained across the MoAu.
elements.sent-2411-062: While the original CLASS \unit{90 {GHz detectors were planned to have $\Tc= 150\mK$, the upgraded design increased the target to $\Tc=190\mK$.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The higher $\Tc$ was chosen to accommodate additional bias power range, matching the more stable \unit{150/220 {GHz detectors \citep{dahal20HF .}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: The rest of the detector wafer is maintained at a ``bath temperature'' $T_{\mathrm{bath }$ of approximately \unit{50 {mK by the helium dilution refrigerator-based receiver during observations \citep{iuliano2018spie .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: After the signal passes the short Si beam, it is dissipated in another stepped-impedance Nb-PdAu termination.
elements.sent-2411-066: As with these latter components, the TES termination and bias filter redesign was part of the detector upgrade striving to increase tolerance to fabrication variation and to reduce susceptibility to radio-frequency interference seen in other CLASS devices.
elements.sent-2411-067: The original bolometer (See Fig.~4 of \citealt{dahal18spie ) used a distributed quarter-wave short and a sub-wavelength PdAu resistive line for signal termination.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Integrated simulations of the original 90 GHz microstrip transition to the sensor termination, its bias choke networks, and the TES enclosure reduced the cavity structure to a 2-port with a mirror symmetry to improve simulation and optimization speed.
elements.sent-2411-069: This approach largely followed the simulation strategy used in modeling the inputs and readout signals for the 40GHz sensor (i.e., where the cavity size is correspondingly smaller compared to the wavelength and enabled higher inter-port isolation).
elements.sent-2411-070: Due to the differences in scaling between the cavity parameters controlling the sensor’s thermal and electromagnetic isolation, this aspect of the design tends to become more challenging as one goes to shorter wavelengths.
elements.sent-2411-071: While the microwave input at the top of Fig. 1d drives the TES cavity symmetrically, bowing of the TES bolometer structures due to membrane stresses or misalignment of elements in hybridization of the TES enclosure can potentially break this symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-072: In reviewing the sensitivity of the CLASS 150/220 GHz pixel design with the electromagnetic simulations it was realized that the model implementation suppressed the possibility of differential modes in the bias circuit shown in the bottom of Fig. 1d.
elements.sent-2411-073: The model was refined to treat the integrated circuit and its packaging structures as a 3-port and used to confirm that excitation of the cavity did not lead to trapped mode resonances~\cite{Morgan2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-074: The 150/220 GHz detector circuitry and its enclosure successfully addressed these considerations~\cite{dahal20HF ).}
elements.sent-2411-075: On the TES island, the Pd thermal mass provides the heat capacity needed to buffer the power load, slowing down the bolometer to maintain device stability.
elements.sent-2411-076: In the original design four distinct Pd regions define the sensor heat capacity and were an integral part of the sensor’s termination and bias choke design.
elements.sent-2411-077: In the revised design, the thermal and electromagnetic functions were intentionally separated by replacing the four distinct Pd regions with a contiguous film that only serves to define the heat capacity of the pixel.
elements.sent-2411-078: To obtain a target heat capacity of \unit{3.7 {pJ/K at $\Tc=190\mK$, an area of \unit{45945 {\mu m^2 of Pd was deposited to a thickness of \unit{400 {nm .\footnote{This assumes a Pd specific heat of $9.4\,\mathrm{mJ/mol/K^2}$ from \cite{kittel96 .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: The MoAu bilayer is the same as in the original design, incorporating the ``zebra-stripe'' normal-metal meander that helps stabilize the TES \citep{Staguhn2004, Ullom-zebra .}
elements.sent-2411-080: The upgraded design incorporates an additional metallic contact extending the zebra stripes to the Pd.
elements.sent-2411-081: This contact further unifies the thermal circuitry of the island, lumping the electronic heat capacity into a single element.
elements.sent-2411-082: Finally, the routing and filtering of voltage leads used for bias and read out of the sensor were revised in the upgraded design.
elements.sent-2411-083: The bias chokes, intended to limit microwave leakage via the bias leads, were extended onto the membrane’s long folded legs and through TES cavity enclosure entrances.
elements.sent-2411-084: Relative to the original design the new filter has more sections, a larger stopband~\cite{U-yen-Filters , and works in concert with the TES cavity enclosure as one of several means of mitigating potential “blue-leak” radiation threats.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Additionally, the original design's bias-lead choke filters, intended to limit microwave leakage via the bias leads, were extended onto the membrane's long, folded legs.

elements.sent-2411-087: In-lab non-optical (``dark'') data were used to measure several basic electrothermal parameters (Section \ref{sec:electrothermal ) and phonon noise (Section \ref{sec:noise ).}}

elements.sent-2411-089: Based on cosmological survey data taken from August to October~2022, we estimated optical time constants (Section \ref{sec:electrothermal ) and noise spectra (Section \ref{sec:noise ) for 10-minute data packages.}}
elements.sent-2411-090: From the survey data, we removed packages for which (1) detectors had anomalous optical response (as indicated by an unusual polarization modulator emission signal), (2) the noise equivalent power (NEP) fluctuated significantly over a contiguous span of data ($<24\,\mathrm{hours}$), (3) wind speeds exceeded \unit{6 {m\,s^{-1 , or (4) optical loading was unusually low ($P_\gamma<3.8\,\mathrm{pW}$) or high ($P_\gamma>5.5\,\mathrm{pW}$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: We also excluded all detectors that did not have an orthogonally polarized counterpart (i.e., ``unpaired'' detectors).\footnote{Estimating detector NEP using the difference of data from two orthogonally-polarized paired detectors mitigates contributions from noise common to the pair.}

elements.sent-2411-271: In this paper, we have reported on the performance of upgraded detectors for the CLASS $90\GHz$ observing band.
elements.sent-2411-272: Four out of seven wafers in the $90\GHz$ detector array were replaced with upgraded versions.
elements.sent-2411-273: Minor changes to the sensor implementation were motivated by lessons learned modeling the 150/220 detector array.
elements.sent-2411-274: For example, the strategy used for termination of the planar OMT’s magic-tee and via-less crossovers was revised.
elements.sent-2411-275: These terminations now use a multi-section sub-wavelength absorptive transformer, which is tolerant to metal properties, proximity effect due to superconducting to normal metal contacts, and fabrication misalignment.
elements.sent-2411-276: The most significant design change was at the TES detector, which now benefits from the same lossy transformer approach for the microwave signal absorption, a reduction in effective enclosure volume, and bias choke filters with higher out-of-band rejection.
elements.sent-2411-277: These electromagnetic and detector electro-thermal circuit modifications (e.g., using a contiguous lumped Pd element on the TES membrane to define the detector speed and stability) have significantly improved the observed performance in the deployed detector arrays.
elements.sent-2411-278: Observations of Jupiter confirm that the telescope optical efficiencies are nearly all above 60\%, implying detector efficiencies in excess of 90\%.
elements.sent-2411-279: The detector $I$--$V$ bias curves show that the TESs on a wafer have a broad range of common bias voltages, with 94\% of detectors stable down to 15\% of the normal resistance.
elements.sent-2411-280: A remaining issue with the detectors is yield in the range of 60\%, which we hypothesize is due to defects on the TES bias leads between each device and the bond pads at the edge of the wafer.
elements.sent-2411-281: Yield can be improved by reducing the length of bias leads (e.g., by routing to multiple edges of the wafer) and by removing design elements and steps that produce defects.
elements.sent-2411-282: In particular, routing the leads to two or four sides of the wafer instead of just one would reduce lead length and densities by one-half to one-fourth, significantly lowering the chances for short or open circuits.
elements.sent-2411-283: This is a straightforward way to increase detector yield for a new CLASS detector module or for future applications of these detectors.
elements.sent-2411-284: We also report detector bandpass and TES electrothermal parameter measurements that match design values except for a lower-than-expected saturation power.
elements.sent-2411-285: Our measurements suggest that this discrepancy would be explained by the thermal conductance prefactor $\kappa$ being lower than expected.
elements.sent-2411-286: However, we found two alternative scenarios in which $\kappa$ matches expectations: the median $\Tc$ may be $10\mK$ higher than measured or the power-law index for heat conduction may be $n=3.77$ instead of the ideal $n=4$ for ballistic phonon transport.
elements.sent-2411-287: The reality is likely some combination of deviations in $\Tc$, $\kappa$, and $n$ from our baseline scenario.
elements.sent-2411-288: Regardless of these assumptions, the resulting lower detector saturation power together with higher optical efficiency results in detectors with significantly lower noise, reducing the array NET from $19\mK\sqrt{\mathrm{s }$ to $11.3\mK\sqrt{\mathrm{s }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-289: The array sensitivity can be further improved by replacing the remaining three original detector wafers with upgraded versions.

elements.subsection-2411-002: Revised Magic Tee and Crossover Terminations
elements.sent-2411-329: For the original 90 GHz detector pixels, absorber terminations for the teminated vialess crossovers and the magic tees were realized in a microstrip configuration by overlaying a superconducting Nb line with a wider resistive Au layer shaped to provide adiabatic absorption along the structure and impedance matching.

elements.sent-2411-332: The new design features a stepped-impedance transformer from superconducting Nb to normal PdAu.
elements.sent-2411-333: Palladium's larger density of states, reduced diffusivity, and strong Pauli paramagnetism combine to limit the proximitization of PdAu relative to that of Au.
elements.sent-2411-334: PdAu is a disordered alloy (RRR=1.1) with a relatively high electrical bulk resistivity, allowing a reduction in the termination footprint.
elements.sent-2411-335: Finally, the stepped-impedance design is more robust against dimensional variations and misalignments in fabrication than the original tapered design.
